turns.text: Hello Dale, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking. It’s… it’s been a lot, honestly. Adjusting to life here in the Bay with two kids when I’m still half-expecting to wake up in that hospital room—it’s hard to put into words.

Most days, I feel like I’m just waiting for the next shoe to drop. People keep telling me, "Oh, you’re in the clear now, you’re home," but they didn't see what I saw. When I was pregnant, the Hepatitis B meant I was always just a medical file to them. Every appointment was about my viral load or the vaccines for the baby. I felt like I had to be the one to remind everyone, "Don't forget the immunoglobulin, don't forget the protocol." It’s exhausting being a mother and a watchdog at the same time.

And then the seizure happened. I don't even remember the transition from being a person to being a "complication." I just remember the fear that I wouldn't be here for my kids. Now, out here in the quiet, the isolation just magnifies it. I check my blood pressure constantly, and every time I get a headache, I’m right back in that hospital bed. I’m just trying to figure out how to stop being so afraid of my own body. Where do we start?
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I think I expected the standard stuff—the exhaustion, the soreness, maybe needing some help around the house. I had this idea in my head that once the baby was out, the "medical" part of my pregnancy would be over. I thought I’d be dealing with healing stitches and sleep deprivation, just the usual "new mom" territory. I was ready for that. I even had a routine planned for how to balance the kids once I was back on my feet.

The reality was nothing like that. It wasn't just physical healing; it was like my body had betrayed me. Because of the eclampsia, I spent weeks feeling like I was walking on eggshells. Every time I stood up too fast, I felt lightheaded, and my heart would just start racing—not because I was tired, but because I was terrified I was going to seize again. I couldn't just "recover" because I didn't trust myself. 

I’m still checking my blood pressure three, sometimes four times a day, just staring at those numbers like they’re going to tell me if I’m safe or not. Nobody told me that the recovery would be ninety percent mental. I’m physically "fine" according to the charts, but I don't feel fine. I feel fragile.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I’ll be honest, feeding was the one thing I thought I could actually control. With everything else—the blood work for the Hep B, the specialists, the constant "what-ifs"—I really wanted this to be that one quiet, natural bridge between me and my baby. I thought it would be this peaceful, bonding thing, even with the fatigue. I imagined sitting in the rocking chair, just us, no needles or clinical talk. 

But when I got home from the hospital after the seizure, everything felt fractured. The anxiety from that trauma made it so hard to just settle in. I was so jumpy, constantly waiting for my head to spin or my blood pressure to spike, and it’s hard to nurse when your body is stuck in 'fight or flight' mode. I didn't feel like a calm, nurturing mother; I felt like a ticking clock. 

The support just wasn't there, either. Out here in Buzzards Bay, the distance makes it impossible to just pop into a clinic for help, and I didn't have anyone nearby to hold the other kid so I could actually focus. It became another task on the list, another thing I was terrified I’d mess up because my body wasn't "behaving" right. It wasn't the peaceful moment I planned; it was just one more source of panic.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I expected to feel the usual "baby blues," maybe some teariness or the overwhelm that comes with a newborn. I had this idea that I’d be tired, sure, but that there would be this overwhelming sense of relief that the pregnancy, with all its medical "what-ifs," was finally done. I expected to feel a sense of accomplishment, like I’d successfully navigated the hurdles and could finally breathe.

The reality? It’s been nothing but a constant, low-grade hum of terror. I don’t feel "postpartum," I feel "post-trauma." The anxiety isn't just about the baby—it’s about *me* surviving. Every time my head throbs or I feel a bit dizzy, I’m not worried about a migraine; I’m worried that I’m going to leave my two children motherless. 

It’s incredibly isolating to have these people look at me and say, "You're a medical success story, your numbers are great," while I’m sitting in my living room, paralyzed by the fear that my own body is out to get me. I’m not just a mom dealing with a newborn; I’m a woman who had to come back from a place where I almost didn't exist anymore. It’s hard to focus on being a parent when you're still mourning the sense of safety you thought you had.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

That’s a heavy question, but yeah, there’s a massive gap. It’s like there’s this unspoken script I’m supposed to follow. Because I’m a "low-risk" patient now—the Hep B is under control, the eclampsia didn't leave permanent organ damage, the baby is healthy—the doctors act like the job is done. They check the boxes, they see a clean bill of health, and they move on. 

I feel like I’m not "allowed" to complain or be scared because, well, I survived, didn't I? Every time I try to mention the fear—the real, raw terror of it—I see them switch back to "clinical mode." They give me that sympathetic smile and tell me to get some sleep or talk to a therapist, but it’s like they’re just checking a box for my mental health, too. 

Even with family, I hide it. I don't want to be the "broken" one. I don't want them looking at me like I’m fragile. So, I put on this mask. I say, "Oh, we're doing great, just tired," while inside, I’m screaming. There’s no space for me to say, "I am terrified of my own body." I’m supposed to be the resilient single mom who handles everything. If I admit how shattered I feel, I’m afraid it’ll make it real.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

Before the baby, I think I defined myself by my independence—especially as a single mom. I was the one who managed the appointments, held down the job, and kept things afloat. I had this idea that I’d just add "mother of two" to that resume. I thought I’d just be "me, but busier." 

But the reality has completely dismantled that. I don’t feel like an independent adult anymore; I feel like a medical patient who hasn’t been discharged yet. My identity feels tethered to my pulse and my blood pressure readings. I’m not "Dale" who works and gets by; I’m "Dale, the high-risk case who survived a seizure." 

It’s like the person I was—the one who could handle a quiet house in Buzzards Bay and feel at peace—is someone I can barely remember. That woman was confident. This version of me is constantly scanning for danger. I feel like a guest in my own life, just hovering near the kids, wondering if I’m going to be there to see them grow up. It’s a terrifying shift. I didn't realize how much of my identity was built on the assumption that I was safe in my own skin, and now that that's gone, I’m not sure who’s looking back at me in the mirror.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

My work—my job—was the biggest one. Before this, my work was my sanctuary. It was where I was "Dale the employee," not "Dale the patient" or "Dale the mother." It was a place where I had value that wasn't tied to my health status or my ability to manage a crisis. I expected to slide back into that, maybe with a few adjustments for the kids, but I felt like I had a handle on it.

Now? I feel like I’ve lost my professional edge. I can’t focus for long stretches because I’m always listening for a cry or checking my blood pressure. But more than that, I feel like I can’t "be" anyone else because my brain is just a constant fire alarm. How am I supposed to talk to coworkers about deadlines or meetings when I’m busy wondering if this dull ache in my head is just stress or a precursor to another seizure? 

Even socially, I’ve just… pulled back. I don’t want to be the "medical project" that everyone feels sorry for. I miss being the person who could just laugh at a joke or talk about the future without looking over my shoulder. It’s hard to hold onto the "old me" when the "new me" is constantly busy just trying to stay conscious and present. I feel like I'm grieving a person who isn't even dead.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected the check-ups to be a place where someone would actually help me put the pieces back together. I thought, "Okay, the hospital was the crisis, but the clinic is where the recovery happens." I expected someone to look at me—not just the chart—and ask how my brain was handling what I’d gone through. I expected them to talk to me about the trauma, or at least acknowledge that having a seizure during birth isn't just a bump in the road.

In practice? It was a conveyor belt. The visits were fast, transactional, and focused entirely on the numbers: "Blood pressure is stable, Hep B numbers are stable, baby is thriving, see you in six weeks." When I tried to bring up how I felt—the panic, the fear of the seizure returning—it was like they didn't know what to do with that information. They’d nod, murmur something about hormones, and then pivot back to the physical vitals. 

It felt like they were managing the *biology*, but they completely ignored the *human*. I left every single appointment feeling smaller and more alone. They gave me clearance to live my life, but they didn't give me the tools to actually feel safe in it. I wasn't a person with a story; I was just a case that didn't need further intervention.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

The biggest one was the headaches. I brought it up at my follow-up, explaining that I was having these sharp, lingering pains and feeling like my head was in a vice. I was terrified it was the pre-eclampsia coming back, or that something was wrong with my brain after the seizure. I wasn't just complaining; I was trying to tell them that I didn't feel safe in my own body.

The response was always the same: "It’s probably just lack of sleep, Dale." Or, "You’re under a lot of stress, that’s normal for a new mom." They’d check my pressure in the office, and because it was okay for those five minutes I was sitting still, they’d act like that was the end of the conversation. They didn't hear the desperation in my voice. They didn't see that I was asking for reassurance, not just a blood pressure cuff.

It made me feel like I was the "hysterical" mother. I stopped bringing it up after a while because I knew the script. If I kept pushing, I was just a nuisance, a "worrier." But the truth is, I *was* worried, and I had every right to be. When your body tries to kill you once, you don't just "relax" because someone in a lab coat tells you your BP is 120/80 for a fleeting moment. That dismissal? That was worse than the medical bills. It made me feel invisible.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I haven't fully returned yet, and honestly, the thought of it is paralyzing. Before the baby, I saw work as my anchor—it was the place where I was in control, where I was just "Dale," not a patient. I thought I’d be eager to get back to that routine, to have an adult conversation, to feel useful again. I expected it to be a challenge, sure, but a *normal* challenge.

The reality of how I feel now is that I’m terrified to leave the kids. It’s not just "mom guilt"—it’s this deep, irrational fear that if I’m not here, I’m not protecting them, or worse, that if something happens to me while I’m away, I won’t be here to keep them safe. And physically? I’m exhausted in a way that sleep doesn't fix. My concentration is shot. I try to look at my laptop and I just feel this fog. 

How can I be a reliable employee when I’m scared to drive the distance to town, or when a sudden noise makes my heart pound like I’m back in that hospital bed? I feel like I’m holding onto the ghost of who I was professionally, but I’m too hollowed out by this experience to actually be that person. I’m just trying to survive the day, and work feels like a mountain I don't have the strength to climb.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I’m a single mother, so I don't have a partner to share the load with. That’s the reality of it. Before the baby arrived, I think I told myself I was strong enough to do this on my own—that I’d just be "super-mom" and handle the house, the kids, the recovery, and the medical stuff all in one stride. I had this picture of myself as this resilient, self-sufficient force.

The truth? It’s crushing. When you’re alone, there is no one to say, "Hey, you look a little pale, let me take the baby," or "Why don't you go lie down while I watch the kids?" When the eclampsia hit, the silence in the house went from being a choice to being a cage. Every night, when I’m alone with the kids, I’m the only one who can hear them, the only one who can check if I’m "okay" or if I’m about to seize again. 

There’s no one to witness the recovery, so I feel like I have to perform being "fine" even when I’m not. If I break down, the whole house breaks down. That pressure—the weight of being the only person in the room—is what keeps me up at night. I didn't realize how much of that "independence" I valued was actually a survival mechanism that’s now burning me out.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

I think I have to correct that—there wasn't a partner there to surprise me, and that’s actually the hardest part of all this. The biggest "surprise" was realizing how much of a luxury it is to have someone else in the house who knows you well enough to see the cracks you're trying to hide. 

I’d always prided myself on my independence, telling everyone I had this—"I’m a single mom by choice, I can do this." I expected to just be an efficient machine. But the real surprise was finding out that you can’t "be independent" during a medical crisis. When the eclampsia hit and I was struggling to recover, the total, crushing silence of a house where I’m the *only* adult was deafening. I thought I was strong, but I was just isolated. 

There’s no one to turn to in the middle of the night to say, "I feel weird, do I look okay?" or "Please, just take the baby for an hour so I can breathe without fear." I didn't realize that my "independence" was actually just a lack of a safety net. Coming home to that quiet space makes the trauma feel bigger because I have to carry every single second of it, all by myself, without a single witness to my healing or my fear.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I think I expected—maybe naively—that "maternity care" meant they were taking care of *me*, the person, not just the pregnancy. I thought it would be a partnership. I expected to feel like I was on a team, where they’d guide me through the Hepatitis B stuff and the birth, and if things went sideways, they’d be there to help me process the wreckage afterwards. I expected to be seen.

The biggest gap is the total lack of "whole-person" care. They treated the Hepatitis B, and they managed the eclampsia, but they left the *human being* standing in the rubble. It was all clinical throughput—data points, viral loads, blood pressure readings—but there was zero space for the terror, the trauma, or the isolation of being a single mother in a rural town. 

I received excellent medical "management," but I didn't receive any *care*. They saved my life, but they didn't help me live it afterwards. I’m out here in the quiet of Buzzards Bay, and the medical system has essentially closed my chart and walked away, while I’m still standing here, shaking. They don't seem to realize that a "successful" delivery isn't the end of the story; for a mom like me, that's just the day the real work—and the real fear—begins.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

I would change the way they "hand off" the patient. Right now, the system treats you like a project that gets completed the moment the baby is delivered and the stitches heal. They need to bridge the gap between "medical patient" and "mother at home." 

I would make it mandatory—not optional, not a flyer in a packet—to have a dedicated postpartum liaison who stays with you for at least the first three months. Someone who doesn't just check blood pressure, but checks *you*. Someone who knows your history, knows you had a traumatic birth, and actually checks in on the emotional and physical reality of being home alone. Right now, the system just kicks you out into the world and assumes you’re fine because the monitors say you’re fine.

I’d want a system that acknowledges that a mother isn't just a container for a baby, but a human who needs to be checked on after the "big event." If I’d had one person—one person who didn't see me as a chart or a case to be closed, but as a mom who needed to know she was safe—it would have changed everything. It’s the difference between being abandoned and being supported. We shouldn't have to fight to be seen *after* we’ve already fought to survive.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think what’s sitting with me most is just this… intense desire to be heard, not just listened to. It’s strange, being a "success story" on paper when I feel so much like a failure in my own head. 

I want to be clear that I’m grateful my kids are here, and that the team in the hospital did what they needed to do to keep us alive. But there’s this assumption that once you survive a high-risk birth, you should just be full of joy. It’s like the medical system demands a "thank you" for saving you, but never asks you how you’re going to carry the trauma of being nearly lost. 

I’m out here in the quiet of Buzzards Bay, and every day is a battle to keep the fear of that seizure from consuming the time I have with my kids. I don't want to be a data point anymore. I don't want to be a chart that says "no further follow-up required." I just want to feel like a mother again, instead of a patient who’s waiting for the floor to fall out from under her. I hope that by you asking these questions, someone, somewhere, finally realizes that the recovery *is* the care. It doesn't end in the delivery room. Thank you for letting me be honest about that.
